title: SMOTE: Creating Synthetic Examples
concept: SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) draws lines between existing minority points and creates new artificial points along those lines (making new friends analogy).

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main risk of oversampling by simply duplicating rare rows?

options.text: The model might memorize the duplicated rows (overfitting) instead of learning general patterns.
It deletes too much of the majority class data.
It makes the model predict decimals instead of categories.

exercises.explain: Correct! Simple duplication leads to memorization. We need a way to create new, unique examples.

cards.title: Connecting the dots
cards.body: If you only know two people who play the oboe, duplicating photos of them doesn't teach a model what other oboe players look like. But if you look at the traits they share and imagine someone in between, you get a new, realistic profile!

cards.title: What is SMOTE?
cards.body: SMOTE stands for Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique. Instead of making exact copies of rare examples, SMOTE looks at the existing rare points, draws imaginary lines between them, and places brand new, artificial points along those lines.

cards.title: Making new friends
cards.body: Imagine you have two friends who love spicy food and live on the same street. A "synthetic" friend would be someone who lives halfway between them and also loves moderately spicy food. They are new, but fit right into the group!

cards.title: Simulating Rare Transactions
cards.body: If we have two fraudulent transactions of $100 and $120 at 2 AM, SMOTE might create a brand new transaction of $110 at 2:05 AM. It is realistic and similar to the real ones, but not an exact clone.

cards.title: Synthetic data is just random noise
cards.body: You might think SMOTE just creates random, fake data points. In reality, it is highly structured. By creating points only between real minority examples, it preserves the natural shape and boundaries of the rare category.

cards.title: Duplication vs. SMOTE
cards.body: How synthetic generation compares to simple cloning:

left.text:
Creates exact clones on top of old points.
Leads to memorization.
No new variety.

right.text:
Draws lines between points and creates new ones in between.
Encourages generalization.
Creates realistic variety.

exercises.prompt: How does SMOTE create new minority examples?

options.text: By drawing lines between existing minority points and placing new points along those lines.
By randomly guessing values from a normal distribution.
By deleting rows from the majority class until they match the minority class.
By swapping the labels of the majority class.

exercises.explain: SMOTE interpolates (creates values) along the line segments connecting existing minority examples.

exercises.prompt: True or false: SMOTE creates exact duplicates of existing minority examples.
exercises.explain: False. SMOTE creates brand new, synthetic examples that are similar but not identical to the originals.

exercises.sentence: SMOTE helps prevent overfitting by creating ___ examples instead of exact copies.
exercises.explain: SMOTE creates synthetic (artificial but realistic) examples to add diversity to the minority class.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes a SMOTE action?

options.text: An algorithm identifies two rare fraud points (one for $500, one for $600) and generates a new artificial fraud point of $550.
An algorithm deletes all transactions under $10 to make the dataset smaller.
An algorithm copies a transaction of $500 ten times.

exercises.explain: Creating a new point ($550) between existing ones ($500 and $600) is synthetic generation, which is SMOTE.

exercises.prompt: You want to train an AI to recognize a rare crop disease. You only have 10 photos of infected leaves. Why is using SMOTE to generate synthetic feature values better than simply duplicating the 10 photos?

options.text: SMOTE creates new variations that help the model generalize, whereas duplication causes the model to memorize the exact 10 photos.
SMOTE automatically converts the images into text files.
Duplication makes the model training process 10 times more expensive.
SMOTE deletes the healthy crop photos to balance the dataset.

exercises.explain: Exactly! SMOTE adds realistic variety, helping the model learn the general characteristics of the disease rather than memorizing the specific pixels of your 10 photos. Next, let's learn how to balance the model's focus without changing the data!
